data.source: Laboratory experiment at BonnEconLab (University of Bonn)
data.wave_or_subset: Main treatments: OS (one-stage) and TS (two-stage); additional treatments: THS (three-stage) and OSL (one-stage lottery)
data.sample_size: 320 total subjects participated across all treatments; 124 in main OS/TS treatments; 100 in OSL
data.notes: One-shot between-subjects design; effort chosen from a discrete set with costs; outcomes subject to random shocks (uniformly distributed); risk-neutrality assumed; strategic equivalence used to match OS to TS first stage; programmed in z-Tree; recruitment via ORSEE.

method.description: The study uses controlled laboratory experiments to compare effort provision in multistage elimination tournaments (TS, THS) versus a strategically equivalent one-stage tournament (OS) and a one-stage lottery (OSL). A simple theoretical model with risk-neutral agents, separable effort costs, and uniformly distributed performance shocks yields Nash equilibrium effort predictions for each stage via backward induction. The experiments test whether observed efforts match these predictions, focusing on early-stage excess effort in multistage settings. Statistical tests compare average efforts across treatments and stages and assess deviations from equilibrium. Additional questionnaires capture personal characteristics for exploratory regressions.
steps: Design a theoretical model of multistage elimination tournaments with risk-neutral agents, separable effort costs, and uniform performance noise; derive equilibrium efforts via backward induction.
Implement laboratory treatments: OS (one-stage), TS (two-stage), THS (three-stage), and OSL (one-stage lottery with similar hierarchy).
Recruit subjects via ORSEE and run sessions in BonnEconLab using z-Tree; use a one-shot between-subjects design.
Present instructions and allow subjects to choose effort from a discrete set with associated costs; in TS/THS, run sequential stages with promotions based on output (effort plus random shock).
Assign wages: in TS/THS, finalists receive whigh (winner) or wmed (runner-up); non-finalists receive wlow; in OS/OSL, rewards are structured to be strategically equivalent to the TS first stage.
Record effort choices by treatment and stage; compute payoffs including random shocks.

Analyze data: compare mean efforts to Nash predictions and across treatments/stages using t-tests, Mann-Whitney U-tests, and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests; pool OS/TS first-stage data for regressions on personal characteristics.
Interpret whether efforts deviate from equilibrium predictions, especially in early multistage rounds; assess potential roles of positional concerns and cognitive factors.
method.models: Theoretical tournament model with symmetric subgame perfect Nash equilibrium solved via backward induction; nonparametric tests (Mann-Whitney U, Wilcoxon signed-rank), t-tests for mean comparisons; regression analysis of first-stage efforts on personal characteristics.
method.independent_variables: Treatment (OS, TS, THS, OSL); stage (first, second, final); wage structure; personal characteristics (competitiveness, risk attitudes, overconfidence, cognitive abilities via CRT, forward-looking behavior, performance in Hit 15).

results.summary: Effort in the one-stage tournament (OS) closely matches Nash predictions. In two-stage (TS) and three-stage (THS) tournaments, subjects exert above-equilibrium effort in early stages. TS first-stage effort exceeds OS effort and declines significantly in the second stage, which aligns with equilibrium predictions. THS replicates the pattern: excess effort in early stages, but final-stage effort matches predictions. In the OSL treatment, average effort is slightly above prediction but not significantly so. Nonparametric tests confirm higher first-stage effort in TS than OS, a significant within-TS decline from stage 1 to stage 2, and excess early-stage efforts in THS. Regressions find limited systematic links between effort and personal traits.
